# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Linear Regression. List any two assumptions of linear regression.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Linear Regression is a **supervised learning algorithm** that models the linear relationship between a dependent variable Y and one or more independent variables X by fitting a linear equation $\hat{Y} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \dots + \beta_n X_n$ to the observed data. The coefficients are estimated using the **Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)** method which minimizes the sum of squared residuals.

**Assumptions (1 mark — any two):**
1. **Linearity:** The relationship between X and Y is linear.
2. **Independence:** Observations are independent of each other.
3. **Homoscedasticity:** Constant variance of error terms.
4. **Normality:** Residuals follow a normal distribution.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**What is R²? Why is it important in linear regression?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** R-squared (Coefficient of Determination) is a statistical measure that represents the **proportion of variance** in the dependent variable that is explained by the independent variable(s). It is calculated as:

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{SSE}{SST} = \frac{SSR}{SST}$$

where $SSE$ is the sum of squared errors and $SST$ is the total sum of squares.

**Importance (1 mark):**
- R² ranges from **0 to 1**.
- A higher R² indicates the model fits the data better.
- An R² of 0.85 means 85% of the variation in Y is explained by X.
- It helps in comparing different models.

---

## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)**

> [!NOTE]
> KTU ESE pattern: Each Part B question has an **internal choice** between two sub-questions. Both alternatives are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the OLS method for estimating the regression coefficients. Derive the formula for the slope $\hat{\beta}_1$ in simple linear regression. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Ordinary Least Squares Method (3 marks):**

The OLS method estimates the unknown parameters ($\beta_0, \beta_1$) in a linear regression model by **minimizing the sum of squared residuals** (SSE).

The objective function is:

$$S(\beta_0, \beta_1) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i)^2$$

By taking partial derivatives with respect to $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ and setting them to zero, we obtain the **normal equations**:

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_0} = -2 \sum (Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial S}{\partial \beta_1} = -2 \sum X_i(Y_i - \beta_0 - \beta_1 X_i) = 0$$

Solving these simultaneously gives the OLS estimators.

**Derivation of $\hat{\beta}_1$ (4 marks):**

From the first equation:

$$\sum Y_i = n\beta_0 + \beta_1 \sum X_i \implies \beta_0 = \bar{Y} - \beta_1 \bar{X}$$

Substituting into the second equation and solving:

$$\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}$$

---

**(b)** Write an R program to fit a simple linear regression model using the `mtcars` dataset (predicting `mpg` from `wt`) and make a prediction for `wt = 3.5`. Show the model summary output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```r
# Load data
data(mtcars)

# Build the linear model
model <- lm(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars)

# Display summary
print(summary(model))

# Predict for wt = 3.5
new_data <- data.frame(wt = 3.5)
predicted_mpg <- predict(model, newdata = new_data)
print(predicted_mpg)
```

**Expected Output (Key Values — 3 marks):**

```
Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t-value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  37.2851     1.8776  19.858  < 2e-16 ***
wt           -5.3445     0.5591  -9.559 1.29e-10 ***

Residual standard error: 3.046 on 30 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.7528,	Adjusted R-squared:  0.7446
F-statistic: 91.38 on 1 and 30 DF,  p-value: 1.294e-10
```

**Prediction (1 mark):** For wt = 3.5: $\hat{mpg} = 37.2851 + (-5.3445)(3.5) \approx 18.58$ mpg.

**Interpretation (3 marks):**
- The negative slope (-5.34) indicates that as weight increases, mileage decreases.
- R² = 0.7528 means **75.28%** of the variation in mpg is explained by weight.
- The p-value < 2e-16 indicates the model is **highly statistically significant**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** What is the difference between **Simple Linear Regression** and **Multiple Linear Regression**? Write the R syntax for both with an example. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Simple Linear Regression | Multiple Linear Regression |
|--------|--------------------------|----------------------------|
| Number of Predictors | One independent variable | Two or more independent variables |
| Equation | $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$ | $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + ... + \beta_n X_n$ |
| Use case | Single feature prediction | Multi-feature prediction |
| Adjusted R² | Same as R² | Uses Adjusted R² |

**R Syntax (4 marks):**

```r
# Simple Linear Regression
slr_model <- lm(mpg ~ wt, data = mtcars)
summary(slr_model)

# Multiple Linear Regression
mlr_model <- lm(mpg ~ wt + hp + disp, data = mtcars)
summary(mlr_model)

# Predictions
predict(slr_model, newdata = data.frame(wt = 3.0))
predict(mlr_model, newdata = data.frame(wt = 3.0, hp = 150, disp = 200))
```

---

**(b)** Explain the following diagnostic plots produced by `plot(model)` in R: (i) Residuals vs Fitted, (ii) Normal Q-Q, (iii) Scale-Location, (iv) Residuals vs Leverage. Why are these plots important? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Residuals vs Fitted (2 marks):**
- Used to check the **linearity** assumption.
- Ideally, residuals should be **randomly scattered** around zero with no pattern.
- A curve or U-shape indicates non-linearity.

**(ii) Normal Q-Q Plot (2 marks):**
- Used to check the **normality** of residuals.
- Points should lie close to the **straight diagonal line**.
- Deviations indicate non-normal residuals.

**(iii) Scale-Location Plot (1.5 marks):**
- Checks **homoscedasticity** (constant variance).
- A horizontal red line indicates equal variance.
- A pattern indicates heteroscedasticity.

**(iv) Residuals vs Leverage (1.5 marks):**
- Identifies **influential observations** (outliers).
- Points outside Cook's distance lines are **high-leverage points** that significantly affect the model.

**Importance (Bonus):** These four plots collectively verify the assumptions of linear regression and help identify model deficiencies.

---

## > [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**

1. **Confusing R² with Adjusted R² (–1 mark):** R² always increases when you add more predictors, even if they are useless. Use **Adjusted R²** for comparing models with different numbers of predictors.
2. **Forgetting `newdata` argument in `predict()` (–2 marks):** `predict(model, newdata = data.frame(wt = 3.5))` — without the `data.frame()` wrapper, R will throw an error.
3. **Misinterpreting p-values (–1 mark):** A p-value > 0.05 does NOT mean the variable is "useless" — it means the evidence is insufficient to reject the null hypothesis at the 5% level.
4. **Not checking assumptions (–2 marks):** KTU examiners specifically look for diagnostic plots or assumption checks. Always validate your model.
5. **Wrong formula in derivation (–3 marks):** Mixing up SSE, SSR, and SST is a very common error. Memorize: **SST = SSR + SSE**.
6. **Confusing `predict()` with `fitted()` (–1 mark):** `fitted()` gives in-sample predictions (for training data). `predict()` is used for new data.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Linear regression** assumes a **linear relationship** between X and Y, fitted using **OLS**.
- The regression line equation is: $\hat{Y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 X$ (simple) or $\hat{Y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 X_1 + \hat{\beta}_2 X_2 + \dots$ (multiple).
- **OLS estimators:** $\hat{\beta}_1 = \dfrac{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})(Y_i - \bar{Y})}{\sum (X_i - \bar{X})^2}$ and $\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{Y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{X}$.
- **Core R functions:** `lm()` (build model), `summary()` (model statistics), `predict()` (new predictions), `plot()` (diagnostics).
- **R² interpretation:** Closer to 1 = better fit. R² = 0.75 means 75% of variance is explained.
- **p-value interpretation:** If p-value < 0.05 → coefficient is **statistically significant**.
- **Five assumptions:** Linearity, Independence, Homoscedasticity, Normality, No Multicollinearity.
- **SSE + SSR = SST** — always remember this decomposition.
- **For predictions:** Always pass a `data.frame` with **same column names** as used in `lm()`.
- **Diagnostic plots:** Residuals vs Fitted, Normal Q-Q, Scale-Location, Residuals vs Leverage — collectively called the "regression quartet."
- **Adjusted R²** is preferred over R² for **multiple linear regression** comparison.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Write an R program to solve logistic regression
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDL508 - DATA ANALYTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Write an R program to solve logistic regression

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Logistic Regression** is a **supervised machine learning classification algorithm** used to predict the probability of a categorical (typically binary) dependent variable $Y \in \{0, 1\}$ given a set of independent variables $X = (X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n)$. It models the log-odds of the outcome as a linear combination of the predictors using the **logistic (sigmoid) function**, and parameters are estimated via **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)**.

In the KTU 2024 Data Analytics Lab, the standard R implementation is performed using the `glm()` function with `family = binomial`, which is the canonical method endorsed by the syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Highlight:** Although the name contains "regression", logistic regression is a **classification algorithm**. Writing "regression algorithm" in your answer sheet will attract negative marking in viva-voce.

> [!NOTE]
> **Binary vs Multinomial:** The standard `glm()` call in R handles **binary classification**. For more than two classes, the syllabus requires **multinomial logistic regression** using the `nnet` package (`multinom()` function).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **medical diagnosis scenario**: A doctor wants to predict whether a patient has diabetes (Yes/No) based on blood sugar level, age, and BMI.

- If we used **Linear Regression**, we would get continuous outputs like $-0.4$ or $1.7$, which are meaningless for a Yes/No question.
- **Logistic Regression** "squashes" the linear output into a probability between **0** and **1** using the **sigmoid function**.

> [!TIP]
> **The "S-Curve" Intuition:** Think of logistic regression as a smooth, S-shaped "switch". When the input is very low, the output is close to 0 (No diabetes). When the input is very high, the output is close to 1 (Yes diabetes). The threshold **0.5** is the "decision boundary" where the switch flips.

**Real-world KTU Exam Examples:**
- **Spam detection** (Spam / Not Spam)
- **Admission prediction** (Admit / Reject)
- **Customer churn** (Churn / Retain)
- **Iris flower species** (Setosa / Versicolor)

The performance metrics standardized by KTU lab rubrics are: **Confusion Matrix, Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and ROC-AUC**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mathematical Foundation — Why a Sigmoid?

Linear regression outputs any real number: $z = \theta^T x \in (-\infty, +\infty)$. We need a function that maps $z$ to a probability in $[0, 1]$. The **sigmoid (logistic) function** is the natural choice because:

1. It is **monotonically increasing**.
2. It is **differentiable everywhere** (essential for gradient-based optimization).
3. It has a clean derivative in terms of itself: $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$.
4. Its **inverse is the logit function**, which is linear — this gives us a beautiful linear-in-parameters model for log-odds.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Concept** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Typical Value** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear Combination | $z = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \cdots + \theta_n x_n$ | Weighted sum of features | $z \in (-\infty, \infty)$ |
| **Sigmoid Function** | $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Maps real number to $(0,1)$ | Output is a probability |
| Hypothesis | $h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x)$ | Predicted probability of $Y=1$ | $h_\theta \in (0, 1)$ |
| **Log-Odds (Logit)** | $\log\left(\dfrac{h_\theta(x)}{1 - h_\theta(x)}\right) = \theta^T x$ | Linear in parameters | Real number |
| **Log-Loss / Cost** | $J(\theta) = -\dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y^{(i)}\log h_\theta(x^{(i)}) + (1-y^{(i)})\log(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)}))\right]$ | Binary cross-entropy | Minimized by MLE |
| **Gradient** | $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)}$ | Used in optimization | Vector form: $\nabla J = X^T(h - y)/m$ |
| **Decision Rule** | $\hat{y} = \mathbb{1}[h_\theta(x) \geq 0.5]$ | Threshold classification | $0.5$ is default |
| **Confusion Matrix** | $\begin{pmatrix} TP & FP \\ FN & TN \end{pmatrix}$ | Evaluation grid | $4$ cells |
| **Accuracy** | $\dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Overall correctness | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Precision** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Positive predictive value | $[0, 1]$ |
| **Recall (Sensitivity)** | $\dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | True positive rate | $[0, 1]$ |
| **F1-Score** | $2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Harmonic mean | $[0, 1]$ |
| **AUC** | $\int_0^1 TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$ | Area under ROC | $[0.5, 1.0]$ ideal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** Use `\vert` or `\mid` in markdown tables. Writing absolute value as $\vert x \vert$ inside table cells prevents the row from breaking.

## 2.3 Why Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)?

Unlike linear regression, logistic regression has **no closed-form solution** (because the sigmoid is non-linear). Hence:

- We construct the **likelihood function** $L(\theta)$ — the probability of observing the data given parameters $\theta$.
- We **maximize** it (or equivalently, minimize the negative log-likelihood, called the **log-loss**).
- R's `glm()` internally calls the **Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS)** algorithm, a Newton-Raphson variant, to find the MLE estimates.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bio-informatics:** Disease classification (cancerous / benign).
- **Finance:** Credit card fraud detection, loan default prediction.
- **Marketing:** Click-through rate (CTR) prediction.
- **Manufacturing:** Defect detection in quality control.
- **Telecommunications:** Customer churn management.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Linear Regression to Sigmoid

**Step 1 — Start with linear regression:**

$$z = \theta^T x = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \cdots + \theta_n x_n$$

**Step 2 — Define the probability using the sigmoid:**

$$P(Y = 1 \mid X) = h_\theta(x) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-\theta^T x}}$$

**Step 3 — Take the odds ratio (Probability / 1-Probability):**

$$\frac{P(Y=1 \mid X)}{P(Y=0 \mid X)} = \frac{h_\theta(x)}{1 - h_\theta(x)} = e^{\theta^T x}$$

**Step 4 — Take the natural logarithm to get the log-odds (logit):**

$$\log\left(\frac{h_\theta(x)}{1 - h_\theta(x)}\right) = \theta^T x$$

This is the **key linearity property** — it is what makes the model interpretable. Each $\theta_j$ represents the change in log-odds for a unit change in $x_j$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Likelihood Estimation

For $m$ independent training samples, the **likelihood function** is:

$$L(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{m} \left[ h_\theta(x^{(i)}) \right]^{y^{(i)}} \left[ 1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)}) \right]^{1 - y^{(i)}}$$

Taking the **logarithm** (log-likelihood):

$$\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h_\theta(x^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log (1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})) \right]$$

**Step 1 — Convert to minimization by negating and averaging (the cost function):**

$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left[ y^{(i)} \log h_\theta(x^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log (1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)})) \right]$$

**Step 2 — Compute the partial derivative with respect to $\theta_j$:**

$$\frac{\partial J(\theta)}{\partial \theta_j} = \frac{1}{m} \sum_{i=1}^{m} \left( h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)} \right) x_j^{(i)}$$

This is the gradient vector used in gradient descent (and in IRLS as used by `glm()`).

## 3.3 Exhaustive R Program — Step-by-Step Implementation

The following is the **canonical R program** expected in a KTU lab examination. Each step is annotated with its purpose and the corresponding mathematical operation.

```r
# ============================================================
# EXPERIMENT: Logistic Regression in R
# Course: PCCDL508 - Data Analytics Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme)
# Module: 2
# ============================================================

# ---- STEP 1: Install and Load Required Libraries ----
install.packages(c("caTools", "caret", "ROCR", "dplyr"))
library(caTools)   # For sample.split()
library(caret)    # For ConfusionMatrix
library(ROCR)     # For ROC curve and AUC
library(dplyr)    # For data manipulation

# ---- STEP 2: Load the Dataset ----
# Using the built-in 'mtcars' dataset for binary classification
# Target: vs (V-shaped engine, 0 = V-engine, 1 = straight engine)
data(mtcars)
df <- mtcars
str(df)
head(df, 5)

# ---- STEP 3: Ensure Target Variable is a Factor ----
# glm() requires a binary outcome to be coded as 0/1 or factor
df$vs <- as.factor(df$vs)
levels(df$vs)  # Should output: "0" "1"

# ---- STEP 4: Split Data into Training and Testing Sets ----
# 80% training, 20% testing — a KTU standard ratio
set.seed(123)  # For reproducibility (mandatory in lab record)
split <- sample.split(df$vs, SplitRatio = 0.8)
train_data <- subset(df, split == TRUE)
test_data  <- subset(df, split == FALSE)

cat("Training samples:", nrow(train_data), "\n")
cat("Testing  samples:", nrow(test_data), "\n")

# ---- STEP 5: Fit the Logistic Regression Model ----
# family = binomial -> triggers logistic regression
model <- glm(vs ~ mpg + wt + hp + disp,
             data = train_data,
             family = binomial)

# ---- STEP 6: Display Model Summary ----
summary(model)
# Examine: Coefficients, p-values, AIC, Null deviance, Residual deviance

# ---- STEP 7: Predict Probabilities on Test Data ----
# type = "response" returns probabilities (NOT logits)
pred_prob <- predict(model, newdata = test_data, type = "response")
print(round(pred_prob, 4))

# ---- STEP 8: Apply Decision Threshold (0.5) ----
pred_class <- ifelse(pred_prob >= 0.5, "1", "0")
pred_class <- as.factor(pred_class)

# ---- STEP 9: Confusion Matrix and Metrics ----
cm <- confusionMatrix(pred_class, test_data$vs, positive = "1")
print(cm)
# Display: Accuracy, Sensitivity, Specificity, Precision, F1

# ---- STEP 10: ROC Curve ----
roc_pred <- prediction(pred_prob, test_data$vs)
roc_perf <- performance(roc_pred, "tpr", "fpr")
plot(roc_perf, main = "ROC Curve - Logistic Regression",
     col = "blue", lwd = 2)
abline(a = 0, b = 1, lty = 2, col = "red")
legend("bottomright", legend = "Logistic Model", col = "blue", lwd = 2)

# ---- STEP 11: Compute AUC (Area Under Curve) ----
auc <- performance(roc_pred, "auc")
auc_value <- as.numeric(auc@y.values)
cat("AUC Value:", round(auc_value, 4), "\n")
# AUC > 0.5 indicates the model is better than random guessing
```

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

| **Step** | **Code Block** | **Mathematical Equivalent** | **Purpose** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `library(caTools)` | — | Load tools for splitting data |
| 2 | `data(mtcars)` | — | Import dataset |
| 3 | `as.factor()` | Encoding $Y \in \{0,1\}$ | Prepare binary outcome |
| 4 | `sample.split()` | $\vert D_{\text{train}} \vert / m = 0.8$ | Create train-test partition |
| 5 | `glm(... binomial)` | MLE via IRLS | Fit parameters $\theta$ |
| 6 | `summary(model)` | Inspect $\hat{\theta}$, $p$-values | Statistical inference |
| 7 | `predict(... response)` | $h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x)$ | Compute $P(Y=1 \mid X)$ |
| 8 | `ifelse(... >= 0.5)` | $\hat{y} = \mathbb{1}[h_\theta \geq 0.5]$ | Threshold classification |
| 9 | `confusionMatrix()` | Compute TP, FP, TN, FN | Evaluate accuracy |
| 10 | `ROCR::prediction()` | TPR vs FPR sweep | Build ROC curve |
| 11 | `performance(... auc)` | $\int_0^1 TPR \, d(FPR)$ | Single-number metric |

## 3.5 Reading the Model Output — Examiner's Checklist

When `summary(model)` is printed in the lab record, students must comment on:

- **(Intercept):** The log-odds of $Y=1$ when all predictors are zero.
- **Coefficients $\theta_j$:** Sign indicates direction of effect; magnitude (after exponentiating) gives the **odds ratio** $e^{\theta_j}$.
- **p-values (Pr(>vert zvert)):** If $p < 0.05$, the feature is statistically significant.
- **Null Deviance vs Residual Deviance:** A large drop indicates good fit.
- **AIC (Akaike Information Criterion):** Lower is better; used for model comparison.
- **Fisher Scoring Iterations:** The number of IRLS iterations needed to converge.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Workflow of Logistic Regression in R

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START Program Execution] --> B[Load Libraries: caTools, caret, ROCR]
    B --> C[Load Dataset: mtcars or CSV]
    C --> D[Preprocess: Convert Target to Factor 0 1]
    D --> E[Set Random Seed for Reproducibility]
    E --> F[Split Data: 80 percent Train, 20 percent Test]
    F --> G[Fit Model: glm formula family binomial]
    G --> H[Estimate Parameters via IRLS MLE]
    H --> I[Display Summary: Coefficients p values AIC]
    I --> J[Predict Probabilities: type response]
    J --> K[Apply Threshold: ifelse prob ge 0.5]
    K --> L[Build Confusion Matrix]
    L --> M[Compute Accuracy Precision Recall F1]
    M --> N[Generate ROC Curve via ROCR]
    N --> O[Calculate AUC Value]
    O --> P[Plot ROC and Print Metrics]
    P --> Q[END]

    style A fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    style G fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    style H fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    style K fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    style L fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    style Q fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
```

## 4.2 The Math-to-Code Bridge: How `glm()` Computes the Hypothesis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Feature Vector x] --> B[Compute Linear Score z equals theta transpose x]
    B --> C[Apply Sigmoid Function: 1 over 1 plus e to power minus z]
    C --> D[Output Probability h theta x in 0 to 1]
    D --> E{Threshold 0.5}
    E -->|Yes| F[Predict Class 1]
    E -->|No| G[Predict Class 0]
    D --> H[Store Probability for ROC Computation]
    H --> I[Vary Threshold from 0 to 1]
    I --> J[Plot TPR versus FPR]
    J --> K[Compute Area Under Curve AUC]

    style C fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    style E fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
    style K fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Confusion Matrix Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[All Test Predictions] --> B[Predicted Positive]
    A --> C[Predicted Negative]
    B --> D[Actually Positive: TP]
    B --> E[Actually Negative: FP Type I Error]
    C --> F[Actually Positive: FN Type II Error]
    C --> G[Actually Negative: TN]

    D --> H[Accuracy equals TP plus TN divided by Total]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    D --> I[Recall equals TP divided by TP plus FN]
    F --> I
    D --> J[Precision equals TP divided by TP plus FP]
    E --> J
    H --> K[F1 equals 2 times Precision Recall divided by Precision plus Recall]
    I --> K
    J --> K

    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000
    style G fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    style K fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Direct Short-Answer)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Set A]**
**Define logistic regression. State the function used to map the linear output to a probability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Logistic regression is a **supervised classification algorithm** used to predict a binary categorical outcome $Y \in \{0, 1\}$ based on one or more independent variables.

The linear output $z = \theta^T x$ is mapped to a probability in $[0, 1]$ using the **sigmoid (logistic) function**:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

The hypothesis is $h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x)$, and a threshold of **0.5** is applied to assign class labels. Parameters are estimated using **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** via the `glm()` function with `family = binomial` in R.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Set B]**
**Write the R command to fit a logistic regression model with target variable `y` and predictors `x1` and `x2` from dataframe `data1`. How is prediction done for a new test sample?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

```r
# Fit the model
model <- glm(y ~ x1 + x2, data = data1, family = binomial)

# Predict on new test data
pred_prob <- predict(model, newdata = test_data, type = "response")
pred_class <- ifelse(pred_prob >= 0.5, 1, 0)
```

The `type = "response"` argument is critical — without it, `predict()` returns the **logit** values instead of probabilities.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**

**(a)** Explain the mathematical model of logistic regression. Derive the sigmoid function and the cost function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete R program to perform logistic regression on the `iris` dataset (using only `setosa` and `versicolor` classes), including data splitting, model fitting, prediction, and confusion matrix evaluation. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Mathematical Model (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Linear Model Foundation (1 mark):**
Start with a linear combination: $z = \theta_0 + \theta_1 x_1 + \theta_2 x_2 + \cdots + \theta_n x_n = \theta^T x$.

**Step 2 — Why Sigmoid? (2 marks):**
Linear regression outputs unbounded real numbers, but we need a **probability** bounded in $[0, 1]$. The sigmoid function:

$$\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$$

satisfies $\sigma(z) \in (0, 1)$ and is differentiable. Key property: $\sigma'(z) = \sigma(z)(1 - \sigma(z))$.

**Step 3 — Hypothesis (1 mark):**
The predicted probability is $h_\theta(x) = \sigma(\theta^T x)$. Decision rule: predict $Y = 1$ if $h_\theta(x) \geq 0.5$, else $Y = 0$.

**Step 4 — Log-Odds Linearity (1 mark):**
Taking $\log\left(\dfrac{h_\theta(x)}{1 - h_\theta(x)}\right) = \theta^T x$ shows log-odds are linear in $\theta$, making coefficients interpretable.

**Step 5 — Cost Function (2 marks):**
Mean-squared-error is non-convex for the sigmoid. The correct cost is the **binary cross-entropy**:

$$J(\theta) = -\frac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}\left[y^{(i)}\log h_\theta(x^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)})\log(1 - h_\theta(x^{(i)}))\right]$$

This is convex and is minimized via MLE / gradient descent. Its gradient is $\dfrac{\partial J}{\partial \theta_j} = \dfrac{1}{m}\sum_{i=1}^{m}(h_\theta(x^{(i)}) - y^{(i)})x_j^{(i)}$.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Complete R Program (7 marks)**

```r
# Load libraries
library(caTools)
library(caret)

# Step 1: Prepare binary dataset from iris
data(iris)
df <- iris[iris$Species %in% c("setosa", "versicolor"), ]
df$Species <- factor(df$Species)  # Binary: setosa/versicolor

# Step 2: Split into training (80%) and testing (20%)
set.seed(42)
split <- sample.split(df$Species, SplitRatio = 0.8)
train_data <- subset(df, split == TRUE)
test_data  <- subset(df, split == FALSE)

# Step 3: Fit logistic regression model
model <- glm(Species ~ Sepal.Length + Sepal.Width +
             Petal.Length + Petal.Width,
             data = train_data, family = binomial)

# Step 4: Examine model
summary(model)

# Step 5: Predict probabilities
pred_prob <- predict(model, test_data, type = "response")

# Step 6: Convert to class labels using 0.5 threshold
pred_class <- ifelse(pred_prob >= 0.5, "versicolor", "setosa")
pred_class <- factor(pred_class, levels = levels(test_data$Species))

# Step 7: Confusion matrix
confusionMatrix(pred_class, test_data$Species)

# Step 8: ROC and AUC
library(ROCR)
roc_pred <- prediction(as.numeric(pred_prob),
                       as.numeric(test_data$Species))
roc_perf <- performance(roc_pred, "tpr", "fpr")
plot(roc_perf, col = "blue", main = "ROC Curve")
abline(a = 0, b = 1, lty = 2, col = "red")
auc <- performance(roc_pred, "auc")@y.values[[1]]
cat("AUC:", round(auc, 4), "\n")
```

**Mark Distribution for Q.A(b):**
- Data preparation: 1 mark
- Train-test split: 1 mark
- `glm()` model fitting: 1 mark
- Prediction with `type = "response"`: 1 mark
- Threshold classification: 1 mark
- Confusion matrix + metrics: 1 mark
- ROC-AUC: 1 mark

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**(a)** Explain the following performance metrics for a logistic regression classifier with formulas: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and AUC. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an R program to load a CSV file (`data.csv`) with a binary target `Outcome`, split the data, fit a logistic regression model using all available predictors, and report the confusion matrix, accuracy, and a plot of the ROC curve. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Performance Metrics (7 marks)**

**1. Confusion Matrix (1 mark):**
A 2×2 table of predictions vs. actuals:

|              | Predicted 1 | Predicted 0 |
|--------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Actual 1** | TP          | FN          |
| **Actual 0** | FP          | TN          |

**2. Accuracy (1 mark):** $\text{Accuracy} = \dfrac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$

**3. Precision (1 mark):** $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ — *"Of those predicted positive, how many are correct?"*

**4. Recall / Sensitivity (1 mark):** $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ — *"Of actual positives, how many did we catch?"*

**5. F1-Score (1.5 marks):** $F_1 = 2 \cdot \dfrac{\text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ — harmonic mean, useful for imbalanced classes.

**6. AUC (Area Under ROC Curve) (1.5 marks):** The ROC plots TPR (Recall) vs FPR at varying thresholds. AUC summarizes this curve as a single number. **AUC = 0.5** means random guessing; **AUC = 1.0** means perfect classifier.

$$AUC = \int_0^1 TPR(FPR) \, d(FPR)$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — CSV-Based R Program (7 marks)**

```r
# Load libraries
library(caTools)
library(caret)
library(ROCR)

# Step 1: Read CSV
df <- read.csv("data.csv")
str(df)

# Step 2: Convert target to factor
df$Outcome <- as.factor(df$Outcome)

# Step 3: Train-test split
set.seed(100)
split <- sample.split(df$Outcome, SplitRatio = 0.75)
train_data <- subset(df, split == TRUE)
test_data  <- subset(df, split == FALSE)

# Step 4: Fit logistic regression (use all predictors via ".")
model <- glm(Outcome ~ ., data = train_data, family = binomial)
summary(model)

# Step 5: Predict probabilities
pred_prob <- predict(model, test_data, type = "response")

# Step 6: Classify at threshold 0.5
pred_class <- ifelse(pred_prob >= 0.5, "1", "0")
pred_class <- as.factor(pred_class)

# Step 7: Confusion matrix and accuracy
cm <- confusionMatrix(pred_class, test_data$Outcome)
print(cm$table)   # Confusion matrix
cat("Accuracy:", cm$overall["Accuracy"], "\n")

# Step 8: ROC plot
roc_pred  <- prediction(pred_prob, test_data$Outcome)
roc_perf  <- performance(roc_pred, "tpr", "fpr")
plot(roc_perf, col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2,
     main = "ROC Curve for Logistic Regression")
abline(a = 0, b = 1, lty = 2, col = "red")

# Step 9: AUC value
auc <- performance(roc_pred, "auc")@y.values[[1]]
cat("AUC:", round(auc, 4), "\n")
```

**Mark Distribution for Q.B(b):**
- Reading CSV: 1 mark
- Train-test split: 1 mark
- Model fitting with `glm(Outcome ~ .)`: 1 mark
- Prediction with `type = "response"`: 1 mark
- Confusion matrix + accuracy: 1 mark
- ROC plot: 1 mark
- AUC calculation: 1 mark

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting `type = "response"`** in `predict()`: This is the **#1 reason** students lose 1 mark. Without it, R returns logits, and your confusion matrix will be 100% wrong.
> 2. **Not converting the target to `as.factor()`**: `glm()` with `family = binomial` requires the target to be 0/1 numeric or a factor. Forgetting this triggers an error.
> 3. **Confusing regression with classification**: Don't report $R^2$ for logistic regression — use **deviance** or **pseudo-$R^2$** (McFadden's).
> 4. **Hard-coding threshold 0.5**: Mention in the answer that the threshold can be tuned using **Youden's J statistic** ($\text{sensitivity} + \text{specificity} - 1$).
> 5. **Not setting `set.seed()`**: Examiners check for reproducibility — always set a seed.
> 6. **Plotting the ROC without the diagonal reference line**: The dashed red line at $y = x$ represents random guessing and **must be included** for full marks.
> 7. **Reporting accuracy alone for imbalanced data**: Always pair it with **F1-score** or **AUC**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Logistic regression is a CLASSIFICATION algorithm**, despite the word "regression" in its name.
- The **sigmoid function** $\sigma(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ squashes any real value to $(0, 1)$.
- The **logit** (log-odds) is linear in $\theta$: $\log\left(\dfrac{p}{1-p}\right) = \theta^T x$.
- The **cost function** is **binary cross-entropy** (log-loss), NOT mean-squared-error.
- R's `glm(formula, data, family = binomial)` is the **standard KTU-approved** implementation.
- **Always use `type = "response"`** in `predict()` to get probabilities.
- **Threshold of 0.5** is the default; can be tuned for precision/recall trade-off.
- The **confusion matrix** is the primary evaluation tool: $\begin{pmatrix} TP & FP \\ FN & TN \end{pmatrix}$.
- **Accuracy alone is misleading** for imbalanced classes — use F1, AUC, or precision-recall.
- **AUC = 0.5** is random; **AUC = 1.0** is perfect; **AUC > 0.8** is generally considered good.
- Parameters are estimated via **MLE** (specifically **IRLS** in R's `glm()`), not OLS.
- Coefficients $\theta_j$ represent the **change in log-odds** per unit change in $x_j$.
- **Exponentiating** $\theta_j$ gives the **odds ratio** $e^{\theta_j}$, a clinician-friendly metric.
- The **diagonal reference line** in the ROC plot represents random guessing and must be included.
- `set.seed()` ensures **reproducibility** — a KTU lab record without it loses 0.5 marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement SVM and Decision Tree Classifier using R
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDL508 - DATA ANALYTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement SVM and Decision Tree Classifier using R

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Support Vector Machine (SVM)

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Support Vector Machine (SVM)** is a supervised machine learning algorithm used for **classification** and **regression** tasks. It works by finding the **optimal hyperplane** in an N-dimensional feature space (where N = number of features) that maximally separates data points belonging to different classes. The data points closest to the hyperplane are called **Support Vectors**, and they directly influence the position and orientation of the hyperplane.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, SVM is a foundational **discriminative classifier** taught as a maximum-margin classification technique within the broader domain of predictive analytics.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Think of SVM like drawing the widest possible road between two rival neighborhoods on a map.** Imagine a city has two groups of houses — the **Red Colony** and the **Blue Colony**. You are asked to build a road (a boundary) that separates them. The *widest* road you can build will give the *maximum safety margin* from both colonies. The houses nearest to this road are the **Support Vectors** — they are the "border guards" that decide where the road goes. If new houses are built later, as long as they don't cross the road, the boundary still works.

### Key Terminology
- **Hyperplane**: The decision boundary separating classes. In 2D, it is a line; in 3D, it is a plane; in N-D, it is a hyperplane.
- **Support Vectors**: The data points lying closest to the hyperplane; they are the most difficult to classify and define the margin.
- **Margin**: The perpendicular distance between the hyperplane and the nearest data point of either class. SVM aims to **maximize** this margin.
- **Kernel Function**: A mathematical trick that maps low-dimensional non-linearly separable data into a higher-dimensional space where it becomes linearly separable.

---

## 1.2 Decision Tree Classifier

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Tree** is a non-parametric supervised learning algorithm used for both **classification** and **regression**. It builds a tree-structured model where each **internal node** represents a test on a feature, each **branch** represents the outcome of that test, and each **leaf node** represents a class label (decision). The tree is constructed using a top-down **greedy approach** known as **Recursive Binary Splitting**, selecting the feature that best splits the data at each step based on metrics like **Entropy**, **Information Gain**, or **Gini Index**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Decision Tree like playing the game "20 Questions" to guess an animal.** You start with all animals (the **root**). First, you ask: *"Is it a mammal?"* If YES, you go down the **left branch**; if NO, the **right branch**. Each question splits the remaining possibilities into smaller groups. By the time you reach a **leaf node** (final question), you have narrowed it down to a single animal. The tree "learns" the *best* questions to ask first by choosing the split that gives the *most information* (highest Information Gain).

### Key Terminology
- **Root Node**: Represents the entire dataset and the first best split.
- **Internal Node / Decision Node**: Represents a test on a single attribute.
- **Branch**: Represents the outcome of a test, leading to a child node.
- **Leaf Node / Terminal Node**: Holds the class label — no further splitting.
- **Pruning**: The process of removing unnecessary branches to prevent **overfitting**.
- **Splitting Criteria**: Entropy, Information Gain, Gini Index, or Chi-Square.

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Algorithms Together in the Lab?

> [!NOTE]
> KTU pairs SVM and Decision Tree in this experiment because they represent **two fundamentally different classification philosophies**:
> - **SVM** is a **geometric / margin-based** classifier — it cares about *distance* from the boundary.
> - **Decision Tree** is a **logical / rule-based** classifier — it cares about *feature thresholds* and information content.
>
> Studying both exposes students to **diverse inductive biases**, a key learning outcome (CO) under the KTU 2024 OBE framework.

### Key R Packages Used

| Package | Purpose | Key Functions |
|---|---|---|
| `e1071` | SVM implementation in R | `svm()`, `tune.svm()` |
| `rpart` | Recursive Partitioning (Decision Trees) | `rpart()`, `predict.rpart()` |
| `caret` | Confusion matrix and model evaluation | `confusionMatrix()` |
| `party` | Conditional inference trees | `ctree()` |
| `rpart.plot` | Visualization of decision trees | `rpart.plot()` |
| `caTools` | Train-test data splitting | `sample.split()` |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 SVM — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.1.1 Linear SVM — The Maximum Margin Hyperplane

For a binary classification problem with training data $\{(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}$ where $y_i \in \{-1, +1\}$, the **decision hyperplane** is defined as:

$$\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{w}$ = weight vector (normal to the hyperplane)
- $b$ = bias term
- $\mathbf{x}$ = feature vector of a data point

The **optimization objective** of SVM is to **minimize** $\|\mathbf{w}\|^2$ subject to the constraint that all points are correctly classified with margin $\geq 1$:

$$y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1, \quad \forall i = 1, 2, \dots, N$$

This is a **constrained quadratic optimization problem**, typically solved using **Lagrange multipliers** $\alpha_i \geq 0$:

$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b, \alpha) = \frac{1}{2} \|\mathbf{w}\|^2 - \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i \left[ y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) - 1 \right]$$

The **dual form** (which is computationally more efficient and allows kernel trick):

$$\max_{\alpha} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i - \frac{1}{2} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{N} \alpha_i \alpha_j y_i y_j K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$$

Subject to: $\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i = 0$ and $\alpha_i \geq 0$.

### 2.1.2 Soft Margin SVM (Handling Non-Separable Data)

In real-world data, perfect separation is rare. **Slack variables** $\xi_i \geq 0$ are introduced:

$$y_i (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i, \quad \forall i$$

The modified objective becomes:

$$\min_{\mathbf{w}, b, \xi} \frac{1}{2} \|\mathbf{w}\|^2 + C \sum_{i=1}^{N} \xi_i$$

Where **C** is the **regularization parameter**:
- **Large C** → strict penalty for misclassification → low bias, high variance (risk of overfitting)
- **Small C** → tolerant of misclassifications → high bias, low variance (risk of underfitting)

### 2.1.3 Kernel Trick — Handling Non-Linear Data

> [!NOTE]
> The **Kernel Trick** is one of the most powerful ideas in ML. Instead of explicitly mapping data $\mathbf{x}$ into a higher-dimensional space $\phi(\mathbf{x})$, the kernel function computes the **dot product** in that high-dimensional space *implicitly*, saving massive computation.

$$K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^T \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$$

### 2.1.4 Common Kernel Functions

| Kernel Type | Formula $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j)$ | When to Use | Hyperparameters |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | $\mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j$ | Linearly separable data, high-dimensional text | None |
| **Polynomial** | $(\gamma \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j + r)^d$ | Image processing, moderate non-linearity | $\gamma$, $r$, $d$ |
| **RBF (Gaussian)** | $\exp(-\gamma \|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\|^2)$ | Most general purpose, non-linear data | $\gamma$ |
| **Sigmoid** | $\tanh(\gamma \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j + r)$ | Neural-network-like behaviour | $\gamma$, $r$ |

Where $\gamma = \frac{1}{2\sigma^2}$ controls the influence radius of a single training example.

---

## 2.2 Decision Tree — Deep Theoretical Analysis

### 2.2.1 Entropy — Measuring Impurity

**Entropy** quantifies the **disorder/impurity** in a dataset. For a node with $C$ classes and $p_i$ = proportion of class $i$:

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2(p_i)$$

Properties:
- $H(S) = 0$ → perfectly pure (all samples in one class)
- $H(S) = \log_2 C$ → maximum impurity (samples equally distributed)
- $H(S) \in [0, \log_2 C]$

### 2.2.2 Information Gain — Measuring Split Quality

**Information Gain (IG)** is the **reduction in entropy** achieved by splitting the dataset $S$ on attribute $A$:

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in Values(A)} \frac{|S_v|}{|S|} H(S_v)$$

Where:
- $H(S)$ = entropy of the original set
- $Values(A)$ = all possible values of attribute $A$
- $S_v$ = subset of $S$ where attribute $A = v$
- $\frac{|S_v|}{|S|}$ = weight of subset $v$

**Decision Tree Algorithm (ID3/C4.5)**: At each node, choose the attribute with the **highest Information Gain** as the splitting attribute.

### 2.2.3 Gini Index — Alternative Impurity Measure

Used by the **CART (Classification and Regression Trees)** algorithm:

$$Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^2$$

**Gini Gain** for split on attribute $A$:

$$\Delta Gini(A) = Gini(S) - \sum_{v \in Values(A)} \frac{|S_v|}{|S|} Gini(S_v)$$

### 2.2.4 Comparison: Entropy vs Gini

| Property | Entropy | Gini Index |
|---|---|---|
| Range | $[0, \log_2 C]$ | $[0, 1 - 1/C]$ |
| Computation | Requires $\log$ → slower | Only squaring → faster |
| Sensitivity to class distribution | More sensitive | Slightly less sensitive |
| Used by | ID3, C4.5 | CART |

### 2.2.5 Overfitting Control in Decision Trees

| Technique | Description |
|---|---|
| **Pre-pruning (Early Stopping)** | Stop tree growth when gain < threshold (`minsplit`, `minbucket`, `maxdepth` in R) |
| **Post-pruning (Cost-Complexity Pruning)** | Grow full tree, then remove branches that don't improve validation accuracy |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|
| SVM Hyperplane | $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ | Decision boundary |
| SVM Primal Objective | $\min \frac{1}{2}\|\mathbf{w}\|^2 + C\sum \xi_i$ | Subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1 - \xi_i$ |
| Margin Width | $\frac{2}{\|\mathbf{w}\|}$ | Maximized when $\|\mathbf{w}\|$ is minimized |
| Linear Kernel | $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j$ | No parameters |
| Polynomial Kernel | $K = (\gamma \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j + r)^d$ | $d$ = degree |
| RBF Kernel | $K = \exp(-\gamma\|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\|^2)$ | Most common, default in `e1071` |
| Entropy | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Use base 2 for bits |
| Information Gain | $IG(S,A) = H(S) - \sum_v \frac{\vert S_v\vert}{\vert S\vert} H(S_v)$ | Higher = better split |
| Gini Index | $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i^2$ | Used by CART |
| Accuracy | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Range $[0, 1]$ |
| Precision | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Class-specific |
| Recall (Sensitivity) | $\frac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Class-specific |
| F1-Score | $\frac{2 \cdot \text{Precision} \cdot \text{Recall}}{\text{Precision} + \text{Recall}}$ | Harmonic mean |
| Specificity | $\frac{TN}{TN + FP}$ | True Negative Rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** For KTU theory questions, always define the metric (e.g., "Information Gain is the *expected reduction in entropy* caused by partitioning the dataset on an attribute") and then write the formula. A common mistake is writing $\log$ without specifying the base.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

| Algorithm | Industry Applications |
|---|---|
| **SVM** | Bioinformatics (cancer classification), Face Detection (Instagram/Tags), Handwriting Recognition (OCR), Spam Filtering, Stock Market Prediction, Remote Sensing Image Classification |
| **Decision Tree** | Medical Diagnosis (rule-based expert systems), Credit Risk Scoring in Banking, Customer Churn Prediction, Fault Diagnosis in Manufacturing, Loan Approval Systems |

# 3. Step-by-Step Implementation in R & Analytical Derivations

## 3.1 Part A: SVM Implementation in R — Full Working Code

### Program: SVM Classifier on the `iris` Dataset

```r
# ============================================
# SVM Classifier Implementation in R
# Dataset: iris (built-in)
# Goal: Classify iris species using SVM
# ============================================

# Step 1: Install and load required packages
install.packages("e1071")    # SVM library
install.packages("caTools")  # Train-test split
install.packages("caret")    # Confusion matrix

library(e1071)
library(caTools)
library(caret)

# Step 2: Load and explore the dataset
data(iris)
cat("Dimensions of iris:", dim(iris), "\n")
cat("Class distribution:\n")
print(table(iris$Species))
cat("First 6 rows:\n")
print(head(iris))

# Step 3: Set seed for reproducibility
set.seed(123)

# Step 4: Split into Training (70%) and Testing (30%) sets
split <- sample.split(iris$Species, SplitRatio = 0.7)
train_data <- subset(iris, split == TRUE)
test_data  <- subset(iris, split == FALSE)

cat("Training samples:", nrow(train_data), "\n")
cat("Testing samples :", nrow(test_data), "\n")

# Step 5: Build the SVM model (default = RBF kernel)
svm_model <- svm(Species ~ ., data = train_data,
                 kernel = "radial",   # RBF/Gaussian kernel
                 cost  = 1,           # Regularization parameter C
                 gamma = 0.5)         # Kernel coefficient

cat("Summary of SVM model:\n")
print(svm_model)
cat("Number of Support Vectors per class:\n")
print(svm_model$nSV)

# Step 6: Make predictions on test set
pred_svm <- predict(svm_model, newdata = test_data)

# Step 7: Evaluate the model
cat("\n--- SVM Confusion Matrix (base R) ---\n")
print(table(Predicted = pred_svm, Actual = test_data$Species))

cat("\n--- SVM Confusion Matrix (caret) ---\n")
cm_svm <- confusionMatrix(pred_svm, test_data$Species)
print(cm_svm)

# Step 8: Tune SVM for best C and gamma
cat("\n--- Tuning SVM (this may take time) ---\n")
tune_result <- tune.svm(Species ~ ., data = train_data,
                        kernel = "radial",
                        cost   = c(0.1, 1, 10, 100),
                        gamma  = c(0.1, 0.5, 1, 2))
print(tune_result)
cat("Best parameters:\n")
print(tune_result$best.parameters)

# Step 9: Retrain with best parameters
best_svm <- svm(Species ~ ., data = train_data,
                kernel = "radial",
                cost   = tune_result$best.parameters$cost,
                gamma  = tune_result$best.parameters$gamma)
best_pred <- predict(best_svm, test_data)

cat("\n--- Tuned SVM Accuracy ---\n")
print(confusionMatrix(best_pred, test_data$Species))
```

### Step-by-Step Walkthrough of Each R Statement

| Line | Purpose | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| `install.packages("e1071")` | Package installation | Downloads SVM library from CRAN |
| `library(e1071)` | Package loading | Makes `svm()` function available |
| `data(iris)` | Load dataset | R's most famous classification dataset (150 rows, 5 cols) |
| `set.seed(123)` | Reproducibility | Ensures the random split is the same every run |
| `sample.split()` | Train-test split | Uses stratified sampling to preserve class ratios |
| `svm(Species ~ ., ...)` | Model training | `Species ~ .` means "predict Species using all other columns" |
| `kernel = "radial"` | Kernel choice | RBF kernel for non-linear classification |
| `cost = 1` | Regularization C | Penalty for margin violations |
| `gamma = 0.5` | RBF coefficient | Higher → tighter fit; lower → smoother boundary |
| `predict(svm_model, test_data)` | Prediction | Returns predicted class labels |
| `confusionMatrix()` | Evaluation | Produces Accuracy, Kappa, Sensitivity, Specificity |
| `tune.svm()` | Hyperparameter tuning | Performs grid search over C and gamma |

---

## 3.2 Part B: Decision Tree Implementation in R — Full Working Code

### Program: Decision Tree on the `iris` Dataset

```r
# ============================================
# Decision Tree Classifier Implementation in R
# Dataset: iris (built-in)
# Goal: Classify iris species using Decision Tree
# ============================================

# Step 1: Install and load required packages
install.packages("rpart")
install.packages("rpart.plot")
install.packages("caTools")
install.packages("caret")

library(rpart)
library(rpart.plot)
library(caTools)
library(caret)

# Step 2: Load and explore the dataset
data(iris)
cat("First 5 rows:\n")
print(head(iris, 5))

# Step 3: Split data
set.seed(123)
split <- sample.split(iris$Species, SplitRatio = 0.7)
train_data <- subset(iris, split == TRUE)
test_data  <- subset(iris, split == FALSE)

# Step 4: Build the Decision Tree model
dt_model <- rpart(Species ~ ., data = train_data,
                  method = "class",        # Classification
                  parms  = list(split = "information"),  # Use Information Gain
                  control = rpart.control(minsplit = 10,    # Min samples to split
                                         minbucket = 5,    # Min samples in leaf
                                         maxdepth = 5,     # Max tree depth
                                         cp = 0.01))       # Complexity parameter

cat("Decision Tree Model Summary:\n")
print(dt_model)
cat("\nVariable Importance:\n")
print(dt_model$variable.importance)

# Step 5: Visualize the decision tree
cat("\n--- Plotting Decision Tree ---\n")
rpart.plot(dt_model, type = 4, extra = 104,
           fallen.leaves = TRUE,
           main = "Decision Tree for Iris Classification")

# Optional fancy plot
# fancyRpartPlot(dt_model)

# Step 6: Display CP table to choose pruning
cat("\n--- Complexity Parameter Table ---\n")
printcp(dt_model)
plotcp(dt_model)

# Step 7: Prune the tree using best cp
optimal_cp <- dt_model$cptable[which.min(dt_model$cptable[,"xerror"]), "CP"]
cat("Optimal CP for pruning:", optimal_cp, "\n")

pruned_tree <- prune(dt_model, cp = optimal_cp)
rpart.plot(pruned_tree, type = 4, extra = 104,
           main = "Pruned Decision Tree")

# Step 8: Make predictions
pred_dt <- predict(pruned_tree, newdata = test_data, type = "class")

# Step 9: Evaluate
cat("\n--- Decision Tree Confusion Matrix ---\n")
cm_dt <- confusionMatrix(pred_dt, test_data$Species)
print(cm_dt)
```

### Detailed Walkthrough of Decision Tree Code

| Function / Argument | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `rpart()` | Builds recursive partitioning tree (CART-like) |
| `method = "class"` | Specifies classification (vs `"anova"` for regression) |
| `parms = list(split = "information")` | Uses **Information Gain** (entropy-based) as split criterion |
| `parms = list(split = "gini")` | Uses **Gini Index** (default in CART) |
| `minsplit = 10` | A node must have ≥ 10 obs to be considered for splitting |
| `minbucket = 5` | Each leaf must contain ≥ 5 observations |
| `maxdepth = 5` | Tree can grow up to 5 levels deep |
| `cp = 0.01` | Complexity parameter; splits that don't improve fit by this much are pruned |
| `rpart.plot()` | Visualizes the tree with class labels and probabilities |
| `printcp()` | Displays CP table used for pruning decision |
| `prune()` | Removes branches that don't improve cross-validated error |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Computing Information Gain by Hand

> [!NOTE]
> **This derivation is a typical KTU exam problem.** A common question is: *"Given the following dataset, compute the Information Gain of splitting on attribute X."*

**Given Dataset (10 samples, target = Plays Tennis):**

| Day | Outlook | Temp | Humidity | Wind | Play |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sunny | Hot | High | Weak | No |
| 2 | Sunny | Hot | High | Strong | No |
| 3 | Overcast | Hot | High | Weak | Yes |
| 4 | Rain | Mild | High | Weak | Yes |
| 5 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 6 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Strong | No |
| 7 | Overcast | Cool | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| 8 | Sunny | Mild | High | Weak | No |
| 9 | Sunny | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 10 | Rain | Mild | Normal | Weak | Yes |

**Step 1: Compute Entropy of Target (Play)**

Counts: Yes = 6, No = 4. Total = 10.

$$H(S) = -\frac{6}{10}\log_2\frac{6}{10} - \frac{4}{10}\log_2\frac{4}{10}$$

Computing each term:
$$\frac{6}{10}\log_2\frac{6}{10} = 0.6 \times (-0.737) = -0.4422$$
$$\frac{4}{10}\log_2\frac{4}{10} = 0.4 \times (-1.322) = -0.5288$$

$$H(S) = -(-0.4422) - (-0.5288) = 0.4422 + 0.5288 = 0.971 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 2: Compute Entropy of each branch after splitting on "Wind"**

- **Wind = Weak**: 6 samples → Yes = 4, No = 2
$$H(\text{Weak}) = -\frac{4}{6}\log_2\frac{4}{6} - \frac{2}{6}\log_2\frac{2}{6} = 0.9183 \text{ bits}$$

- **Wind = Strong**: 4 samples → Yes = 2, No = 2
$$H(\text{Strong}) = -\frac{2}{4}\log_2\frac{2}{4} - \frac{2}{4}\log_2\frac{2}{4} = 1.0 \text{ bit}$$

**Step 3: Compute Weighted Average Entropy after split**

$$H(S \mid \text{Wind}) = \frac{6}{10}(0.9183) + \frac{4}{10}(1.0) = 0.5510 + 0.4000 = 0.9510 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4: Compute Information Gain**

$$IG(S, \text{Wind}) = H(S) - H(S \mid \text{Wind}) = 0.971 - 0.951 = 0.020 \text{ bits}$$

This is a **very low IG**, so "Wind" is a poor splitter for this dataset — the algorithm would prefer splitting on "Outlook" (which yields IG ≈ 0.246 bits).

## 3.5 Practical Scenarios — When to Use Which?

| Scenario | Recommended Classifier | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| High-dimensional, small sample (e.g., gene expression data, ~1000 features, ~100 samples) | **SVM** with linear kernel | SVM excels in high-D sparse settings |
| Need to explain decision to a non-technical stakeholder (e.g., loan officer) | **Decision Tree** | Produces interpretable IF-THEN rules |
| Data has complex non-linear boundaries, no clear feature threshold | **SVM** with RBF kernel | Captures non-linear patterns well |
| Mixed data types (categorical + numerical) | **Decision Tree** | Handles both without one-hot encoding |
| Speed of prediction is critical (real-time systems) | **Decision Tree** | $O(\log N)$ lookup vs SVM's $O(N \cdot d)$ |
| Image classification (e.g., MNIST) | **SVM** (RBF) | Classical state-of-the-art before deep learning |
| Risk of overfitting on noisy data | **SVM** with proper C tuning | Strong regularization control |

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Raw Training Data xy pairs] --> B[Compute Pairwise Kernel Matrix K]
    B --> C[Solve Quadratic Optimization Max Sum alpha minus 1 2 Sum Sum alpha i alpha j y i y j K]
    C --> D[Identify Support Vectors alpha i greater than 0]
    D --> E[Compute w and b from Support Vectors]
    E --> F[Define Decision Function f x sign w T x plus b]
    F --> G[Test Point x new] --> H[Apply Kernel Compute f x] --> I[Predicted Class Label]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Recursive Splitting Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Root Node Entire Dataset S] --> B[For Each Attribute Compute IG or Gini Gain]
    B --> C[Pick Attribute with Highest Gain]
    C --> D[Split S into S1 S2 Sk branches]
    D --> E{For each subset check stopping criteria}
    E -- minsplit met and gain greater than threshold --> B
    E -- minsplit not met or pure node --> F[Create Leaf Node Assign Majority Class]
    F --> G[Optional Pruning cp table xerror]
    G --> H[Final Pruned Tree]
```

```mermaid
    matrix --> M2[Precision TP divided by TP plus FP]
    matrix --> M3[Recall TP divided by TP plus FN]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Root Petal Length less than 2.45] -->|Yes| B[Leaf Setosa]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define Support Vector Machine (SVM). What are support vectors?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Support Vector Machine (SVM) is a **supervised machine learning algorithm** used for classification and regression that finds the **optimal hyperplane** which maximizes the **margin** between two or more classes in an N-dimensional feature space. The hyperplane is defined as $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$ where $\mathbf{w}$ is the weight vector and $b$ is the bias.

**Support vectors** are the data points that lie **closest to the separating hyperplane** — they are the most difficult to classify and directly determine the position and orientation of the hyperplane. If these points are moved, the hyperplane changes; if all other points are moved (as long as they stay outside the margin), the hyperplane is unaffected. Mathematically, these correspond to training samples with non-zero Lagrange multipliers $\alpha_i > 0$.

In R, SVM is implemented using the `svm()` function from the `e1071` package: `svm(Species ~ ., data = train, kernel = "radial")`.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]
**Explain the concept of Information Gain in Decision Tree construction. Write its formula.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Information Gain (IG)** is a statistical measure used in Decision Tree algorithms (like **ID3** and **C4.5**) to determine the **best attribute** to split the dataset at each node. It is defined as the **reduction in entropy** (or impurity) achieved by partitioning the data on a particular attribute. The attribute with the **highest Information Gain** is selected as the splitting attribute at each step, leading to a tree where each split provides the maximum information about the target class.

The formula is:

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in Values(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

Where $H(S)$ is the entropy of the original set $S$, and $S_v$ represents the subset of $S$ where attribute $A$ takes the value $v$. A higher IG value indicates that splitting on attribute $A$ produces purer child nodes. In R, this is invoked by setting `parms = list(split = "information")` in the `rpart()` function.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Question A(a) [7 marks]: With a neat diagram, explain the working of the Support Vector Machine (SVM) algorithm for a binary classification problem. Discuss the role of the kernel trick in handling non-linearly separable data.**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction to SVM:**
Support Vector Machine is a **discriminative classifier** that finds the decision boundary (hyperplane) that separates data points of different classes with the **maximum margin**.

**Linear Separable Case (4 marks):**

Consider a 2D feature space with two classes (+1 and -1). The decision boundary is a line: $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = 0$.

The two parallel margin boundaries are:
- $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = +1$ (passes through support vectors of class +1)
- $\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = -1$ (passes through support vectors of class -1)

**The margin width** between them is $\frac{2}{\|\mathbf{w}\|}$. SVM minimizes $\|\mathbf{w}\|^2$ subject to $y_i(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x}_i + b) \geq 1$ for all training points.

**Support Vectors:** The critical data points lying on the margin boundaries that determine the hyperplane.

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph space [2D Feature Space with SVM Hyperplane]
        C1[Class plus1 Support Vectors] ---|margin 2 divided by w| H[Hyperplane wT x plus b equals 0]
        H ---|margin 2 divided by w| C2[Class minus1 Support Vectors]
    end
```

**Non-Linear Case and Kernel Trick (3 marks):**

When data is not linearly separable (e.g., concentric circles), the **kernel trick** maps the data into a **higher-dimensional space** where it becomes linearly separable, **without explicitly computing** the transformation $\phi(\mathbf{x})$.

The kernel function $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^T \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$ allows the SVM to operate in this high-dimensional space efficiently.

Common kernels:
- **Polynomial**: $K = (\gamma \mathbf{x}_i^T \mathbf{x}_j + r)^d$
- **RBF (Gaussian)**: $K = \exp(-\gamma \|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\|^2)$ — most widely used

**In R**, the kernel is specified as `kernel = "radial"` or `kernel = "polynomial"` in the `svm()` function.

---

**Question A(b) [7 marks]: Write an R program to build an SVM classifier on the `iris` dataset. Show the confusion matrix and interpret the accuracy, precision, and recall obtained.**

**Model Solution:**

```r
# R Program: SVM on iris dataset
install.packages("e1071")
install.packages("caTools")
install.packages("caret")
library(e1071); library(caTools); library(caret)

data(iris)
set.seed(123)
split <- sample.split(iris$Species, SplitRatio = 0.7)
train_data <- subset(iris, split == TRUE)
test_data  <- subset(iris, split == FALSE)

# Build SVM with RBF kernel
svm_model <- svm(Species ~ ., data = train_data,
                 kernel = "radial", cost = 1, gamma = 0.5)

# Predictions
pred <- predict(svm_model, test_data)

# Confusion matrix and statistics
cm <- confusionMatrix(pred, test_data$Species)
print(cm)
```

**Sample Output (expected on iris):**

```
Confusion Matrix:
            Reference
Prediction   setosa versicolor virginica
  setosa        15         0         0
  versicolor     0        14         1
  virginica      0         1        14

Overall Statistics:
         Accuracy : 0.9556
         95% CI   : (0.8485, 0.9946)
         Kappa    : 0.9333
```

**Interpretation (3 marks):**

- **Accuracy (95.56%)**: The SVM correctly classifies ~96% of all test samples.
- **Precision** (per class): $TP / (TP + FP)$ — e.g., for *versicolor*: $\frac{14}{15} = 93.3\%$, meaning when the model predicts *versicolor*, it is correct 93.3% of the time.
- **Recall** (per class): $TP / (TP + FN)$ — for *versicolor*: $\frac{14}{15} = 93.3\%$, meaning it correctly identifies 93.3% of all actual *versicolor* flowers.
- **Kappa (0.93)**: Strong agreement beyond chance.

The **single misclassification** between *versicolor* and *virginica* is expected — these two species are notoriously difficult to distinguish using only petal measurements.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]

**Question B(a) [7 marks]: With a neat diagram, explain the construction of a Decision Tree using the ID3 algorithm. Discuss how Information Gain and Entropy are used in the splitting process.**

**Model Solution:**

**ID3 Algorithm Overview (3 marks):**

ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3) builds the tree in a **top-down, greedy** manner:

1. Calculate the **entropy** of the target attribute for the current dataset.
2. For each attribute, compute the **Information Gain** if the dataset were split on that attribute.
3. Choose the attribute with the **highest Information Gain** as the splitting attribute.
4. Create a decision node on that attribute and recursively build subtrees for each branch.
5. Stop when:
   - All samples in a node belong to the same class → **leaf node**.
   - No remaining attributes to split on.
   - Splitting does not improve the entropy (gain < threshold).

**Entropy and Information Gain (3 marks):**

$$H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{C} p_i \log_2(p_i)$$

$$IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum_{v \in Values(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$$

**Example:** Splitting on "Wind" with weak (6 samples: 4 Yes, 2 No) and strong (4 samples: 2 Yes, 2 No):
- $H(\text{Weak}) = 0.918$, $H(\text{Strong}) = 1.0$
- $H(S \mid \text{Wind}) = 0.6 \times 0.918 + 0.4 \times 1.0 = 0.951$
- $IG(\text{Wind}) = 0.971 - 0.951 = 0.020$ — *low gain, poor split*

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Root: All Samples Compute H S] --> B[For Each Attribute A compute IG S A]
    B --> C[Pick Attribute A star with max IG]
    C --> D[Create Decision Node A star]
    D --> E[For each value v of A star recurse on subset S v]
    E --> F{Stopping Criteria Met}
    F -- Yes --> G[Create Leaf with Majority Class]
    F -- No --> B
```

**Overfitting and Pruning (1 mark):**

Full tree growth may cause overfitting. **Pre-pruning** uses parameters like `minsplit`, `minbucket`, `maxdepth`. **Post-pruning** (cost-complexity) removes branches with high `xerror` from the `printcp()` table.

---

**Question B(b) [7 marks]: Write a complete R program to build a Decision Tree classifier on the `iris` dataset using the `rpart` package. Show the tree visualization and confusion matrix. Explain the role of `minsplit`, `minbucket`, and `maxdepth` parameters.**

**Model Solution:**

```r
# R Program: Decision Tree on iris dataset
install.packages("rpart")
install.packages("rpart.plot")
install.packages("caTools")
install.packages("caret")
library(rpart); library(rpart.plot); library(caTools); library(caret)

data(iris)
set.seed(123)
split <- sample.split(iris$Species, SplitRatio = 0.7)
train_data <- subset(iris, split == TRUE)
test_data  <- subset(iris, split == FALSE)

# Build Decision Tree
dt_model <- rpart(Species ~ ., data = train_data,
                  method = "class",
                  parms  = list(split = "information"),
                  control = rpart.control(minsplit = 10,
                                         minbucket = 5,
                                         maxdepth = 5,
                                         cp = 0.01))

# Visualize
rpart.plot(dt_model, type = 4, extra = 104,
           main = "Decision Tree - Iris Dataset")

# Pruning
printcp(dt_model)
best_cp <- dt_model$cptable[which.min(dt_model$cptable[,"xerror"]), "CP"]
pruned <- prune(dt_model, cp = best_cp)

# Predictions and evaluation
pred <- predict(pruned, test_data, type = "class")
cm <- confusionMatrix(pred, test_data$Species)
print(cm)
```

**Role of Hyperparameters (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Function | Default | Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| `minsplit` | Minimum number of observations in a node required to attempt a split | 20 | Higher value → smaller, less-overfit tree |
| `minbucket` | Minimum number of observations allowed in any terminal (leaf) node | 7 (round(minsplit/3)) | Ensures leaves are statistically significant |
| `maxdepth` | Maximum depth of any node from root | 30 | Limits tree size; prevents exponential growth |

**Interpretation of Output:**

- The **first split** in the iris tree is on `Petal.Length < 2.45`, perfectly separating the *setosa* class — a hallmark of how Information Gain correctly identifies the most informative feature.
- Confusion matrix typically shows 95–97% accuracy for the iris dataset.
- `variable.importance` ranks `Petal.Width` and `Petal.Length` as the top contributors, matching the well-known statistical finding that petal features discriminate iris species better than sepal features.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in SVM/Decision Tree Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Information Gain" with "Gain Ratio"** — Gain Ratio normalizes IG by the split's intrinsic information (`SplitInfo = -Σ (|Sv|/|S|) log2(|Sv|/|S|)`). ID3 uses raw IG; **C4.5 uses Gain Ratio**. Examiners deduct marks for this mix-up.
>
> 2. **Wrong kernel formula in SVM** — Writing RBF as $\exp(-\|x\|^2)$ instead of $\exp(-\gamma \|x_i - x_j\|^2)$ loses 1 mark; $\gamma$ is **mandatory**.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to standardize features for SVM** — SVM is **distance-based**; unscaled features (e.g., one in $[0,1]$, another in $[0, 10000]$) cause the model to be dominated by the larger-scale feature. Always `scale()` the data first.
>
> 4. **Decision Tree overfitting** — Students often submit a fully grown tree without `printcp()` or `prune()`. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for ignoring overfitting control.
>
> 5. **Using `predict()` without `type = "class"` for rpart** — By default, `predict.rpart` returns **class probabilities** (a data frame), not labels. Forgetting `type = "class"` will crash the confusion matrix.
>
> 6. **Hand-derivation mistakes in Entropy** — Using $\ln$ (natural log) instead of $\log_2$. The formula requires $\log_2$ unless otherwise specified.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for SVM and Decision Tree in R:**

### SVM — Key Points
- **SVM** is a **margin-maximizing classifier** that finds the optimal hyperplane in feature space.
- **Support vectors** are the boundary-defining points with $\alpha_i > 0$.
- **Margin width** = $\frac{2}{\|\mathbf{w}\|}$; maximize margin ↔ minimize $\|\mathbf{w}\|^2$.
- **Soft margin** uses slack variables $\xi_i$ and regularization parameter **C** (large C = strict, small C = tolerant).
- **Kernel trick** maps data implicitly to higher dimensions: $K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)^T \phi(\mathbf{x}_j)$.
- **Four kernels**: Linear, Polynomial, RBF (Gaussian), Sigmoid.
- **RBF** is the most common default: $K = \exp(-\gamma \|\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\|^2)$.
- **In R**: `library(e1071); svm(Species ~ ., data = train, kernel = "radial", cost = 1, gamma = 0.5)`.
- **Tuning**: `tune.svm()` does grid search over C and gamma.
- **Always scale features** before SVM (use `scale()` function in R).

### Decision Tree — Key Points
- **Decision Tree** is a flowchart-like structure; root → internal nodes → leaves.
- **Entropy**: $H(S) = -\sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ — measures impurity (0 = pure, max = equally distributed).
- **Information Gain**: $IG(S, A) = H(S) - \sum \frac{|S_v|}{|S|} H(S_v)$ — used by ID3.
- **Gini Index**: $Gini(S) = 1 - \sum p_i^2$ — used by CART.
- **ID3** uses Information Gain; **C4.5** uses Gain Ratio; **CART** uses Gini.
- **Overfitting control**: Pre-pruning (`minsplit`, `minbucket`, `maxdepth`) and post-pruning (cost-complexity using `printcp` and `prune`).
- **In R**: `library(rpart); rpart(Species ~ ., data = train, method = "class", parms = list(split = "information"))`.
- **Visualization**: `rpart.plot(model, type = 4, extra = 104)`.
- **Prediction**: `predict(model, test, type = "class")` — must specify `type = "class"`.
- **Tree interpretation**: First split has highest IG; `variable.importance` ranks features.

### Evaluation Metrics — Key Points
- **Accuracy** = $(TP + TN) / \text{Total}$ — overall correctness.
- **Precision** = $TP / (TP + FP)$ — quality of positive predictions.
- **Recall** = $TP / (TP + FN)$ — coverage of actual positives.
- **F1-Score** = harmonic mean of Precision and Recall.
- **Confusion Matrix** is the foundation of all classification metrics.
- **Kappa** adjusts accuracy for agreement by chance; > 0.8 = excellent.

### Practical R Workflow
1. `install.packages()` and `library()`.
2. `data()` and `head()` / `str()` to explore.
3. `set.seed()` and `sample.split()` for reproducible train-test split.
4. Build model: `svm()` or `rpart()`.
5. Tune: `tune.svm()` or `printcp()` + `prune()`.
6. `predict()` on test set.
7. `confusionMatrix()` from `caret` for evaluation.
8. Compare and conclude.

### Final Exam One-Liners
- *"SVM maximizes margin; Decision Tree maximizes information gain."*
- *"Kernel trick: implicit high-D dot product without explicit transformation."*
- *"Gini is faster; Entropy is more theoretically pure."*
- *"Always scale features for SVM; not strictly required for Decision Trees."*
- *"In R, `type = 'class'` is mandatory for rpart predictions used in confusion matrices."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Implement KNN and Naive Bayes Classifier using R
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCDL508 - DATA ANALYTICS LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Implement KNN and Naive Bayes Classifier using R

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN)** is a non-parametric, instance-based supervised learning algorithm used for classification and regression. For a given test sample, KNN identifies the *K* closest training samples in the feature space (using a distance metric such as Euclidean distance) and assigns the class label by majority voting among those *K* neighbors.

**Naive Bayes Classifier** is a probabilistic supervised learning algorithm based on Bayes' Theorem, which assumes strong (naive) independence between every pair of features given the class label. Despite this strong assumption, it performs remarkably well on high-dimensional datasets such as text classification.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme Data Analytics Laboratory, these two classifiers are implemented using the **R programming language** by leveraging standard libraries such as `class` (for KNN) and `e1071` (for Naive Bayes).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Experiment 2 specifically requires the student to *implement, train, test, and evaluate* both KNN and Naive Bayes on a standard dataset (commonly the `iris` dataset or a custom CSV file), then report accuracy using a **Confusion Matrix**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**KNN Analogy – "Tell me who your friends are, and I will tell you who you are"**
Imagine a new student joins a college. We don't know which stream (Science/Arts/Commerce) they belong to. We look at the **5 nearest students** sitting in the canteen. If 4 of them are from Science, we predict the new student is also from Science. That is exactly what KNN does in feature space!

**Naive Bayes Analogy – "A Doctor's Diagnostic Reasoning"**
A doctor sees symptoms (fever, cough, sore throat). She knows the prior probability of flu during winter. She then updates this prior based on each independent symptom using Bayes' rule to compute the probability of flu. The "naive" part is the assumption that symptoms occur independently, which is mathematically convenient and works surprisingly well in practice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **KNN** is a **lazy learner** (no training phase, all work happens at prediction time).
> - **Naive Bayes** is an **eager learner** (builds a probabilistic model during training, then predicts instantly).

### 1.3 Important Metrics Highlighted in Bold

- **Euclidean Distance (d):** Standard distance metric, $d = \sqrt{\sum (x_i - y_i)^2}$
- **K (n_neighbors):** Number of nearest neighbors considered.
- **Prior Probability $P(C)$:** The baseline likelihood of a class.
- **Posterior Probability $P(C \mid X)$:** The updated probability of class $C$ given evidence $X$.
- **Confusion Matrix:** A 2x2 (for binary) or NxN (for multi-class) table summarizing TP, TN, FP, FN.
- **Accuracy:** The ratio of correct predictions to total predictions.
- **Laplace Smoothing (alpha):** A correction term added to handle zero-frequency problems in Naive Bayes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept of KNN – Step-by-Step Logic

1. Choose a value of **K** (number of neighbors).
2. Compute the **Euclidean distance** between the new test point and every point in the training set.
3. Sort all training points by their distance in ascending order.
4. Select the top **K** closest training points (the K-nearest neighbors).
5. Conduct a **majority vote** among these K neighbors — the most frequent class label is assigned to the test point.
6. Return the predicted class.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why scale/normalize features?** Because Euclidean distance is sensitive to the magnitude of features. If one feature ranges 0–1000 and another 0–1, the larger-magnitude feature will dominate. Hence, `scale()` or `normalize()` is applied in R before KNN.

### 2.2 Operational Concept of Naive Bayes – Step-by-Step Logic

1. Compute the **prior probability** $P(C)$ for each class from the training data.
2. Compute the **likelihood** $P(X \mid C)$ for each feature value, assuming independence.
3. Apply **Bayes' Theorem** to compute the **posterior** $P(C \mid X)$ for each class.
4. Predict the class with the **highest posterior probability**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / R Function | Purpose |
|---|---------|----------------------|---------|
| 1 | Euclidean Distance | $d = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - y_i)^2}$ | Distance metric in KNN |
| 2 | Bayes' Theorem | $P(C \mid X) = \frac{P(X \mid C) \cdot P(C)}{P(X)}$ | Core of Naive Bayes |
| 3 | Naive Independence Assumption | $P(X \mid C) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid C)$ | Simplifies computation |
| 4 | Accuracy | $\frac{TP + TN}{TP + TN + FP + FN}$ | Performance metric |
| 5 | Precision | $\frac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Positive predictive value |
